summary: Unclear weight between strategic housing need and local restrictive criteria leads to inconsistent refusals and appeals.
Validation requirements differ materially between LPAs, creating repeat submissions and front-end delay.
Local plan and supplementary guidance repeat overlapping design tests with slight wording differences, causing officer interpretation drift.
Environmental assessment sequencing often duplicates evidence requests across regimes, extending determination timelines.
Transitional policy periods create uncertainty where emerging local plans are given differing weight across authorities.
No nationally consistent required-document checklist means validation officers apply local interpretation, generating avoidable invalid notices.
Housing needs assessment methodologies vary between neighbouring authorities, producing incompatible housing land supply figures that undermine cross-boundary cooperation.
LURA 2023 plan-making provisions not yet fully commenced, creating a dual-track system where authorities operate under transitional rules of varying vintage.
Section 106 negotiation timelines are unregulated post-resolution, causing delays of 6-18 months between committee approval and legal completion.
Statutory consultee responses overlap in scope between highway authority, Environment Agency, and Historic England, with no coordination protocol to prevent contradictory requirements.
Habitats Regulations Assessment and EIA can require near-identical ecological survey data via separate legal routes, doubling applicant cost and extending pre-determination period.
Pre-commencement condition discharge applications attract no statutory determination period under current rules, routinely extending post-permission delay by 3-12 months.
Transport assessment scope and methodology is negotiated case-by-case with highway authorities, producing inconsistent requirements for comparable commercial schemes.
NPPF 2024 grey belt policy introduces a new land category without clear boundary criteria, generating uncertainty about what constitutes green belt harm for decision-making.
EIA screening thresholds have not been updated to reflect current development typologies, resulting in over-screening of smaller schemes and under-screening of cumulative impacts.
Viability assessments required at plan-making stage are routinely re-opened at application stage, duplicating cost and creating policy uncertainty for developers.
Statutory consultee response periods are not enforced; non-responses routinely extend consultation windows beyond 21-day statutory period without consequence.
Boundary between NSIP and non-NSIP thresholds is contested for hybrid infrastructure schemes, triggering jurisdictional uncertainty between PINS and LPA determination.
LPAs apply inconsistent affordable housing tenure mix requirements in s106, creating renegotiation risk and delaying legal completion.
Pre-commencement conditions are frequently imposed by LPAs beyond NPPF/PPG guidance necessity tests, increasing post-permission workload without commensurate planning benefit.
NSIP pre-application consultation requirements are disproportionate for smaller nationally significant schemes, adding 12-24 months before examination can commence.
Decision-makers apply tilted balance inconsistently when housing land supply is below five-year requirement, producing divergent outcomes for identical scheme types.
